Judicial Review.rule: Judicial review is the power of the federal courts to review the constitutionality of legislative and executive actions.
Judicial Review.application: Because the court reviewed the constitutionality of the statute, judicial review applies.

Supremacy Clause.rule: The Supremacy Clause provides that federal law is the supreme law of the land and prevails over conflicting state law.
Supremacy Clause.application: Because federal law conflicts with state law, federal law controls.

Standing — General Rule.rule: To have standing, a plaintiff must show injury in fact, causation, and redressability.
Standing — General Rule.application: Because the plaintiff suffered a concrete injury, standing exists.

Standing — Injury in Fact.rule: An injury in fact must be concrete, particularized, and actual or imminent.
Standing — Injury in Fact.application: Because the injury was actual and personal, the requirement is met.

Standing — Causation.rule: Causation requires that the injury be fairly traceable to the challenged conduct.
Standing — Causation.application: Because the injury resulted from the statute, causation is satisfied.

Standing — Redressability.rule: Redressability requires that a favorable court decision is likely to remedy the injury.
Standing — Redressability.application: Because the court can provide relief, redressability exists.

Ripeness.rule: A claim is ripe when it presents a real and immediate dispute appropriate for judicial resolution.
Ripeness.application: Because enforcement is imminent, the claim is ripe.

Mootness.rule: A case is moot when intervening events eliminate the controversy between the parties.
Mootness.application: Because the dispute no longer exists, the case is moot.

Mootness — Exceptions.rule: Mootness does not apply when the issue is capable of repetition yet evading review.
Mootness — Exceptions.application: Because the issue is likely to recur, the exception applies.

Political Question Doctrine.rule: Courts will not decide political questions that are constitutionally committed to another branch of government.
Political Question Doctrine.application: Because the issue is committed to Congress, the court will not decide it.

Federal Powers — Enumerated Powers.rule: Congress may act only pursuant to powers enumerated in the Constitution.
Federal Powers — Enumerated Powers.application: Because Congress acted under an enumerated power, the law is valid.

Necessary and Proper Clause.rule: Congress may enact laws that are necessary and proper to carry out its enumerated powers.
Necessary and Proper Clause.application: Because the law is a means to execute an enumerated power, it is constitutional.

Commerce Clause — General Rule.rule: Congress may regulate channels of interstate commerce, instrumentalities of interstate commerce, and activities that substantially affect interstate commerce.
Commerce Clause — General Rule.application: Because the activity substantially affects interstate commerce, Congress may regulate it.

Commerce Clause — Economic Activity.rule: Congress may regulate intrastate economic activity that in the aggregate substantially affects interstate commerce.
Commerce Clause — Economic Activity.application: Because the activity is economic and cumulative effects are substantial, regulation is valid.

Commerce Clause — Noneconomic Activity.rule: Congress may not regulate noneconomic activity based solely on its cumulative effect on interstate commerce.
Commerce Clause — Noneconomic Activity.application: Because the activity is noneconomic, Congress lacks authority.

Taxing Power.rule: Congress may tax and spend for the general welfare.
Taxing Power.application: Because the tax promotes the general welfare, it is valid.

Spending Power — Conditions.rule: Congress may attach conditions to federal funding if the conditions are related to the purpose of the spending and are not unduly coercive.
Spending Power — Conditions.application: Because the condition was related and non-coercive, it is constitutional.

Tenth Amendment.rule: The Tenth Amendment reserves to the states powers not delegated to the federal government.
Tenth Amendment.application: Because the power is not delegated, it belongs to the states.

Anti-Commandeering Doctrine.rule: Congress may not compel states to enact or administer federal regulatory programs.
Anti-Commandeering Doctrine.application: Because the law commandeers state officials, it is unconstitutional.

State Police Power.rule: States possess police power to regulate for the health, safety, welfare, and morals of their citizens.
State Police Power.application: Because the law promotes public safety, it is a valid exercise of police power.

Dormant Commerce Clause — General Rule.rule: State laws may not discriminate against or unduly burden interstate commerce.
Dormant Commerce Clause — General Rule.application: Because the law burdens interstate commerce, it may be invalid.

Dormant Commerce Clause — Discrimination.rule: State laws that discriminate against out-of-state commerce are presumptively invalid unless narrowly tailored to serve an important state interest.
Dormant Commerce Clause — Discrimination.application: Because the law discriminates against out-of-state businesses, it is invalid.

Dormant Commerce Clause — Market Partici.rule: When a state acts as a market participant, it may favor its own citizens over others.
Dormant Commerce Clause — Market Partici.application: Because the state acted as a participant, favoritism is permitted.

Privileges and Immunities Clause — Artic.rule: States may not discriminate against citizens of other states with respect to fundamental rights or important economic activities.
Privileges and Immunities Clause — Artic.application: Because the law discriminates against nonresidents, it violates the clause.

Preemption — Supremacy Clause.rule: Federal law preempts state law when Congress expressly preempts, occupies the field, or when state law conflicts with federal law.
Preemption — Supremacy Clause.application: Because federal law conflicts with state law, the state law is preempted.

Separation of Powers — General Principle.rule: The Constitution divides federal power among the legislative, executive, and judicial branches to prevent the concentration of power.
Separation of Powers — General Principle.application: Because power is divided among branches, no single branch may dominate.

Congressional Power — Legislation.rule: Congress has the exclusive power to enact legislation subject to constitutional limitations.
Congressional Power — Legislation.application: Because Congress passed the statute through bicameralism and presentment, it is valid.

Bicameralism and Presentment.rule: A law must be passed by both houses of Congress and presented to the President for approval or veto.
Bicameralism and Presentment.application: Because the statute was not presented to the President, it is invalid.

Legislative Veto.rule: Congress may not invalidate executive action through a legislative veto without bicameralism and presentment.
Legislative Veto.application: Because Congress used a legislative veto, the action is unconstitutional.

Delegation of Legislative Power.rule: Congress may delegate legislative authority if it provides an intelligible principle to guide the exercise of the delegated power.
Delegation of Legislative Power.application: Because Congress provided standards, the delegation is valid.

Executive Power — General.rule: The President has the power to execute the laws and conduct foreign affairs.
Executive Power — General.application: Because the President acted to enforce federal law, the action is valid.

Executive Orders.rule: The President may issue executive orders pursuant to constitutional or statutory authority.
Executive Orders.application: Because the order was authorized by statute, it is valid.

Presidential Power — Youngstown Framewor.rule: Presidential power is strongest when acting with congressional authorization, weaker when Congress is silent, and weakest when acting against congressional will.
Presidential Power — Youngstown Framewor.application: Because the President acted against Congress, authority is weakest.

Appointment Power.rule: Principal officers must be appointed by the President with Senate confirmation, while inferior officers may be appointed without Senate confirmation.
Appointment Power.application: Because the officer was a principal officer, Senate confirmation was required.

Removal Power.rule: The President generally has the power to remove executive officers, subject to limited congressional restrictions.
Removal Power.application: Because removal restrictions interfered with executive power, they are invalid.

Impeachment.rule: Federal officials may be impeached by the House of Representatives and convicted by the Senate for treason, bribery, or other high crimes and misdemeanors.
Impeachment.application: Because the House impeached and the Senate convicted, removal was proper.

Judicial Power — Federal Courts.rule: The judicial power of the United States is vested in the Supreme Court and inferior federal courts established by Congress.
Judicial Power — Federal Courts.application: Because Congress created the lower courts, judicial power is properly exercised.

Judicial Review of Executive Action.rule: Federal courts may review executive actions for constitutionality and compliance with federal law.
Judicial Review of Executive Action.application: Because the executive action exceeded authority, the court may invalidate it.

Limits on Judicial Power.rule: Congress may limit the jurisdiction of federal courts, but may not dictate the outcome of particular cases.
Limits on Judicial Power.application: Because Congress attempted to control case outcomes, the statute is unconstitutional.

Equal Protection Clause — General Rule.rule: The Equal Protection Clause requires that similarly situated persons be treated alike by the government.
Equal Protection Clause — General Rule.application: Because the law treats similarly situated persons differently, equal protection analysis applies.

State Action Requirement.rule: The Equal Protection Clause applies only to government action and not to purely private conduct.
State Action Requirement.application: Because the discrimination was caused by the government, the clause applies.

Levels of Scrutiny — Overview.rule: Equal protection challenges are analyzed under strict scrutiny, intermediate scrutiny, or rational basis review depending on the classification.
Levels of Scrutiny — Overview.application: Because the classification is suspect, heightened scrutiny applies.

Strict Scrutiny — Standard.rule: Under strict scrutiny, the government must show the law is narrowly tailored to achieve a compelling government interest.
Strict Scrutiny — Standard.application: Because the law is not narrowly tailored, it fails strict scrutiny.

Strict Scrutiny — Suspect Classification.rule: Strict scrutiny applies to classifications based on race, national origin, and alienage.
Strict Scrutiny — Suspect Classification.application: Because the classification is race-based, strict scrutiny applies.

Strict Scrutiny — Fundamental Rights.rule: Strict scrutiny applies when a law burdens a fundamental right such as voting, interstate travel, or access to the courts.
Strict Scrutiny — Fundamental Rights.application: Because the law burdens voting, strict scrutiny applies.

Intermediate Scrutiny — Standard.rule: Under intermediate scrutiny, the government must show the law is substantially related to an important government interest.
Intermediate Scrutiny — Standard.application: Because the law is not substantially related, it fails intermediate scrutiny.

Intermediate Scrutiny — Gender.rule: Classifications based on gender are subject to intermediate scrutiny.
Intermediate Scrutiny — Gender.application: Because the classification is gender-based, intermediate scrutiny applies.

Intermediate Scrutiny — Illegitimacy.rule: Classifications based on illegitimacy are subject to intermediate scrutiny.
Intermediate Scrutiny — Illegitimacy.application: Because the law discriminates based on illegitimacy, intermediate scrutiny applies.

Rational Basis Review — Standard.rule: Under rational basis review, a law is upheld if it is rationally related to a legitimate government interest.
Rational Basis Review — Standard.application: Because the law is rationally related to a legitimate interest, it is constitutional.

Rational Basis — Default Rule.rule: Rational basis review applies to all classifications not subject to strict or intermediate scrutiny.
Rational Basis — Default Rule.application: Because no suspect or quasi-suspect class is involved, rational basis applies.

Facial Discrimination.rule: A law that discriminates on its face is subject to the level of scrutiny appropriate for the classification used.
Facial Discrimination.application: Because the law explicitly classifies by race, strict scrutiny applies.

Discriminatory Intent.rule: A facially neutral law violates equal protection only if it has a discriminatory impact and discriminatory intent.
Discriminatory Intent.application: Because discriminatory intent was shown, the law violates equal protection.

Affirmative Action — Race.rule: Government affirmative action programs using race are subject to strict scrutiny.
Affirmative Action — Race.application: Because the program uses race, strict scrutiny applies.

Affirmative Action — Education.rule: Diversity in higher education may be a compelling interest if race is used in a narrowly tailored manner.
Affirmative Action — Education.application: Because race was one factor among many, the program may be constitutional.

One Person, One Vote.rule: The Equal Protection Clause requires electoral districts to be substantially equal in population.
One Person, One Vote.application: Because districts were malapportioned, equal protection was violated.

Due Process Clause — General Rule.rule: The Due Process Clause prohibits the government from depriving any person of life, liberty, or property without due process of law.
Due Process Clause — General Rule.application: Because the government deprived a protected interest, due process applies.

Procedural Due Process — Requirement.rule: Procedural due process requires fair procedures when the government deprives a person of life, liberty, or property.
Procedural Due Process — Requirement.application: Because a protected interest was taken, procedural safeguards are required.

Protected Interests — Liberty.rule: Liberty interests include freedom from bodily restraint and significant restrictions on personal freedom.
Protected Interests — Liberty.application: Because physical freedom was restricted, a liberty interest is implicated.

Protected Interests — Property.rule: Property interests include legitimate claims of entitlement created by existing rules or understandings.
Protected Interests — Property.application: Because the benefit was guaranteed by law, a property interest exists.

Procedural Due Process — What Process Is.rule: The amount of process due depends on the private interest affected, the risk of erroneous deprivation, and the government’s interest.
Procedural Due Process — What Process Is.application: Because the risk of error was high, greater procedural protection was required.

Notice and Hearing.rule: Procedural due process generally requires notice and an opportunity to be heard before deprivation.
Notice and Hearing.application: Because no hearing was provided, due process was violated.

Timing of Hearing.rule: A hearing may be held after deprivation if prompt post-deprivation review is provided and pre-deprivation process is impracticable.
Timing of Hearing.application: Because immediate action was necessary, a post-deprivation hearing was sufficient.

Substantive Due Process — General Rule.rule: Substantive due process limits the government’s ability to infringe on fundamental rights regardless of the procedures used.
Substantive Due Process — General Rule.application: Because a fundamental right was burdened, substantive due process applies.

Fundamental Rights.rule: Fundamental rights include marriage, privacy, contraception, family relationships, child rearing, and interstate travel.
Fundamental Rights.application: Because the law interferes with marriage, a fundamental right is implicated.

Substantive Due Process — Standard of Re.rule: Laws infringing fundamental rights are subject to strict scrutiny.
Substantive Due Process — Standard of Re.application: Because strict scrutiny applies, the law must be narrowly tailored.

Substantive Due Process — Non-Fundamenta.rule: Laws affecting non-fundamental rights are upheld if they are rationally related to a legitimate government interest.
Substantive Due Process — Non-Fundamenta.application: Because the law is rational, it is constitutional.

Economic Substantive Due Process.rule: Economic regulations are reviewed under rational basis scrutiny.
Economic Substantive Due Process.application: Because the regulation is rationally related to economic policy, it is valid.

Vagueness Doctrine.rule: A law is unconstitutionally vague if it fails to give fair notice of prohibited conduct or encourages arbitrary enforcement.
Vagueness Doctrine.application: Because the statute lacks clear standards, it is void for vagueness.

Overbreadth Doctrine.rule: A law is unconstitutionally overbroad if it prohibits a substantial amount of protected conduct.
Overbreadth Doctrine.application: Because the law restricts protected activity, it is overbroad.

Freedom of Speech — General Rule.rule: The First Amendment prohibits the government from abridging freedom of speech.
Freedom of Speech — General Rule.application: Because the government restricted speech, First Amendment analysis applies.

State Action Requirement — Speech.rule: The First Amendment applies only to government action and not to private conduct.
State Action Requirement — Speech.application: Because the restriction was imposed by the government, the First Amendment applies.

Content-Based Regulation.rule: Content-based regulations of speech are subject to strict scrutiny.
Content-Based Regulation.application: Because the law targets speech based on content, strict scrutiny applies.

Content-Neutral Regulation.rule: Content-neutral regulations of speech are subject to intermediate scrutiny.
Content-Neutral Regulation.application: Because the law regulates time, place, and manner, intermediate scrutiny applies.

Prior Restraint.rule: Prior restraints on speech are presumptively unconstitutional.
Prior Restraint.application: Because the government required approval before speech, the restraint is invalid.

Time, Place, and Manner Restrictions.rule: Time, place, and manner restrictions must be content-neutral, narrowly tailored, serve an important government interest, and leave open alternative channels of communication.
Time, Place, and Manner Restrictions.application: Because alternative channels remain open, the restriction may be valid.

Public Forum — Traditional.rule: Traditional public forums include streets and parks, where content-based restrictions are subject to strict scrutiny.
Public Forum — Traditional.application: Because the speech occurred in a park, strict scrutiny applies.

Public Forum — Designated.rule: A designated public forum is government property opened for expressive activity and is subject to the same standards as a traditional public forum.
Public Forum — Designated.application: Because the government opened the forum, strict scrutiny applies.

Public Forum — Limited or Nonpublic.rule: In limited or nonpublic forums, regulations must be reasonable and viewpoint neutral.
Public Forum — Limited or Nonpublic.application: Because the rule is reasonable and neutral, it is constitutional.

Unprotected Speech — Categories.rule: Unprotected speech includes incitement, obscenity, fighting words, defamation, and true threats.
Unprotected Speech — Categories.application: Because the speech falls into an unprotected category, it may be restricted.

Incitement.rule: Incitement is speech directed to producing imminent lawless action and likely to produce such action.
Incitement.application: Because the speech urged immediate violence, it is unprotected.

Obscenity — Miller Test.rule: Obscenity is defined by whether the average person would find the work appeals to prurient interest, depicts sexual conduct in a patently offensive way, and lacks serious value.
Obscenity — Miller Test.application: Because the material lacks serious value, it is obscene.

Commercial Speech.rule: Commercial speech that is lawful and not misleading is protected but subject to intermediate scrutiny.
Commercial Speech.application: Because the advertisement was truthful, it receives protection.

Symbolic Speech.rule: Symbolic conduct is protected as speech if the conduct is intended to convey a message and the message is likely to be understood.
Symbolic Speech.application: Because the conduct conveyed a message, it is protected speech.

Hate Speech.rule: Hate speech is generally protected unless it falls into an unprotected category such as incitement or true threats.
Hate Speech.application: Because the speech did not threaten violence, it is protected.

Religion Clauses — General Rule.rule: The First Amendment prohibits the government from establishing religion or prohibiting the free exercise of religion.
Religion Clauses — General Rule.application: Because the government action involves religion, First Amendment analysis applies.

Establishment Clause — General Rule.rule: The Establishment Clause prohibits government action that endorses, advances, or excessively entangles the government with religion.
Establishment Clause — General Rule.application: Because the government endorsed religion, the Establishment Clause is implicated.

Establishment Clause — Lemon Test.rule: Under the Lemon test, a law must have a secular purpose, a primary effect that neither advances nor inhibits religion, and must not foster excessive government entanglement with religion.
Establishment Clause — Lemon Test.application: Because the law lacks a secular purpose, it violates the Establishment Clause.

Establishment Clause — Government Aid to.rule: Government aid to religion is permitted if it is neutral, available to a broad class of recipients, and does not promote religion.
Establishment Clause — Government Aid to.application: Because aid was neutrally distributed, it is constitutional.

Establishment Clause — Religious Display.rule: Religious displays are constitutional if a reasonable observer would not perceive the display as government endorsement of religion.
Establishment Clause — Religious Display.application: Because the display conveyed a secular message, it is permissible.

Free Exercise Clause — General Rule.rule: The Free Exercise Clause prohibits the government from substantially burdening religious exercise without sufficient justification.
Free Exercise Clause — General Rule.application: Because the law burdens religious practice, free exercise analysis applies.

Free Exercise — Neutral Laws of General.rule: Neutral laws of general applicability that incidentally burden religion are constitutional.
Free Exercise — Neutral Laws of General.application: Because the law is neutral and generally applicable, it is valid.

Free Exercise — Targeting Religion.rule: Laws that target religious conduct are subject to strict scrutiny.
Free Exercise — Targeting Religion.application: Because the law targets religion, strict scrutiny applies.

Religious Exemptions.rule: The government is not required to grant religious exemptions from neutral laws of general applicability.
Religious Exemptions.application: Because the law applies equally to all, no exemption is required.

Government Employment and Religion.rule: The government may not condition public employment on religious belief or practice.
Government Employment and Religion.application: Because employment was conditioned on religion, the action is unconstitutional.

Religious Speech in Public Schools.rule: Government-sponsored prayer in public schools violates the Establishment Clause.
Religious Speech in Public Schools.application: Because the school sponsored prayer, the Establishment Clause was violated.
